title: The Traitor Within the Keep
subtitle: Insider Threat — Privileged Data Theft
description: A trusted member of your inner council has turned traitor. Under cover of darkness, they have plundered the realm's most sacred records — the identities and fortunes of your most valued patrons. As the treachery unravels, the conspiracy may reach higher than anyone expected.

roles.name: Incident Commander
roles.title: War Chief
roles.description: Leads the response team, makes containment and escalation decisions

roles.name: IT Operations Lead
roles.title: Arcane Engineer
roles.description: Provides technical context, reviews access logs, proposes containment

roles.name: Compliance / DPO
roles.title: Keeper of the Codex
roles.description: Assesses regulatory notification obligations, advises on data breach classification

roles.name: Legal Counsel
roles.title: Loremaster
roles.description: Advises on employment law, evidence preservation, potential prosecution

roles.name: HR Representative
roles.title: Guild Master
roles.description: Manages employee handling, disciplinary procedures, interview coordination

roles.name: Senior Management
roles.title: High Council Elder
roles.description: Provides executive decision authority, manages organisational accountability implications

roles.name: Customer Relations
roles.title: Herald of the Realm
roles.description: Manages customer communication if notification is required

injects.title: Anomalous Activity Detected — The First Whisper
injects.narrative:
The Data Loss Prevention (DLP) system has generated an alert. A senior data analyst with privileged access to the Customer Relationship Management (CRM) system has downloaded an unusually large dataset overnight. The download occurred at 02:17 UTC and comprised the full customer database extract, including: names, addresses, dates of birth, passport numbers, account valuations, account documentation, and transaction holdings for all 1,247 major customers.

The analyst's role requires access to individual customer records but not bulk exports. The DLP policy was configured to alert on downloads exceeding 500 records. The analyst's workstation shows the data was saved to an encrypted USB device.

HR records indicate the analyst submitted their resignation three days ago, with a four-week notice period. Their LinkedIn profile, updated yesterday, lists a new role at a competing organisation starting next month.
facilitator_prompts: What is the appropriate initial response? Do we immediately revoke the analyst's access?
If we revoke access, do we risk alerting the analyst and giving them time to destroy evidence or move data?
What are the legal considerations around monitoring an employee's activity without their knowledge?
Should we involve HR and Legal before taking any technical action?
Is this a data breach under GDPR if the data has not yet left the organisation?
What is the organisation's policy on USB device usage? Should this have been blocked by endpoint controls?

dice_events.description: Roll to determine how the analyst responds to increased scrutiny

critical_fail.title: The Traitor Flees
critical_fail.description: The analyst notices unusual activity on their account monitoring dashboard (they have SOC access through a previous role that was never revoked). They immediately leave the building with their laptop and USB device.
critical_fail.modifier: Analyst has fled with evidence. Laptop and USB unrecoverable. Remote wipe fails.

fail.title: Evidence Destroyed
fail.description: The analyst, sensing something is wrong, performs a secure wipe of the USB device during their lunch break. The data still exists in their personal email but the USB evidence is lost.
fail.modifier: USB evidence destroyed. Must rely on network logs and email traces.

partial.title: Status Quo
partial.description: The analyst continues working normally, unaware of the investigation. The team has time to plan their approach, but every hour increases the risk of data being moved externally.

success.title: Evidence Preserved
success.description: IT quietly takes a forensic image of the analyst's workstation during a planned 'IT maintenance window'. The USB device is still in the analyst's desk drawer.
success.modifier: Forensic image captured. Evidence chain intact.

critical_success.title: Critical Success — The Full Picture
critical_success.description: The forensic image reveals the analyst has been systematically accessing customer records outside their assigned scope for 6 weeks. Browser history shows they have been in contact with a data broker on the dark web. This is bigger than a departing employee — it's organised data theft for sale.
critical_success.modifier: Full scope of theft discovered. Criminal enterprise identified.

random_complications: The analyst's line manager is on annual leave and cannot be reached for context on the analyst's recent behaviour.
The DLP alert was generated 6 hours ago but was missed by the overnight SOC team due to alert fatigue.
The analyst is the nephew of a board member, creating a potential conflict of interest in the investigation.
The organisation's USB blocking policy was approved but never implemented due to a delayed endpoint management rollout.

injects.title: Investigation Findings — The Plot Thickens
injects.narrative:
A covert review of the analyst's email (authorised by Legal and the DPO under the organisation's Acceptable Use Policy) reveals the following:

The analyst sent an encrypted ZIP file to a personal email address at 03:04 UTC on the same night as the download. The file size is consistent with the extracted dataset.

Additionally, a review of the analyst's access logs over the past 30 days shows a pattern of querying individual major customer records that fall outside their assigned scope. Eleven of the queried customers are known to be prospects of the competing organisation the analyst is joining.

The analyst is scheduled to be in the office today and is currently at their desk.
facilitator_prompts: Data has now left the organisation via personal email. What are the regulatory notification obligations?
How do we handle the analyst? Do we confront them, conduct a formal interview, or escalate to law enforcement?
What evidence preservation steps are required before any confrontation?
Is this a matter for the police under the Computer Misuse Act 1990 or the Data Protection Act 2018?
Do we have grounds for an injunction against the competing organisation to prevent use of the stolen data?
Which major customers need to be notified and what do we tell them?

dice_events.description: Roll to determine how the interview with the analyst proceeds

critical_fail.title: Legal Catastrophe
critical_fail.description: The analyst's personal solicitor arrives within minutes — they were already retained in anticipation of this. The solicitor challenges the legality of the email review and threatens an employment tribunal for constructive dismissal and breach of privacy. The investigation is thrown into legal chaos.
critical_fail.modifier: Legal counter-claim filed. Investigation paused pending legal review.

fail.title: Denial and Deflection
fail.description: The analyst denies everything and claims the download was for a legitimate customer review project. They demand union representation and refuse to surrender the USB device without a court order.
fail.modifier: Analyst uncooperative. Court order required for USB. Delay expected.

partial.title: Partial Admission
partial.description: The analyst admits to the download but claims it was for personal reference to 'hit the ground running' at the new role. They downplay the severity and offer to delete all copies voluntarily.
partial.modifier: Partial admission obtained. Voluntary deletion claim cannot be verified.

success.title: Full Cooperation
success.description: Confronted with the evidence, the analyst cooperates fully. They surrender the USB device, provide access to the personal email account, and confirm no other copies exist. They agree to a supervised deletion process.
success.modifier: Full cooperation. Evidence recovered. Supervised deletion in progress.

critical_success.title: Critical Success — The Bigger Fish
critical_success.description: The analyst breaks down and reveals they were recruited by a headhunter working for the competing organisation, who specifically requested customer data as a condition of the job offer. The analyst provides written communications proving the competing organisation's complicity.
critical_success.modifier: Competing organisation's involvement proven. Grounds for regulatory referral and civil action.

random_complications: The competing organisation's CISO contacts your organisation, claiming they have discovered the stolen data on the analyst's application materials and want to 'do the right thing'.
An anonymous tip is received suggesting the analyst is not the only employee who has been approached by the competing organisation.
The analyst's workstation is a shared hot-desk, complicating the forensic chain of custody.
A journalist contacts the organisation asking about 'staff poaching and data theft' — suggesting a leak from somewhere.

injects.title: Complications — The Conspiracy Deepens
injects.narrative:
During a discreet meeting with HR and Legal, the analyst becomes confrontational and claims they were acting on verbal instructions from their line manager (a senior director) to prepare a 'customer transition plan' for the competing organisation as part of an informal arrangement.

The senior director denies any such instruction. However, the analyst produces a text message thread (on a personal device) that contains ambiguous language from the director, including: 'Let's make sure we have everything ready for the move' and 'Keep this between us for now.'

The senior director is a key member of the leadership team.
facilitator_prompts: How does the involvement of a senior director change the response and escalation pathway?
What are the implications for organisational accountability? Do we need to notify the regulator about potential conduct issues?
Should both individuals be suspended pending investigation?
How do we preserve the text message evidence on the analyst's personal device?
Does this change the nature of the offence from a lone actor to potential conspiracy?
What independent investigation capability do we engage to avoid conflicts of interest?

dice_events.description: Roll to determine what the deeper investigation reveals

critical_fail.title: Institutional Crisis
critical_fail.description: The investigation reveals the director has been systematically moving customers to the competing organisation for 18 months. Three other departing staff members were involved. The scale of customer data exposure is ten times larger than initially thought.
critical_fail.modifier: Systemic breach. Multiple perpetrators. Full regulatory investigation triggered.

fail.title: Ambiguous Evidence
fail.description: The text messages are genuinely ambiguous. The director's explanation is plausible but so is the analyst's. Neither can be conclusively proven without further investigation. Both parties lawyer up.
fail.modifier: Stalemate. Expensive and lengthy investigation required.

partial.title: Director Partially Cleared
partial.description: Digital forensics of the director's devices shows no evidence of direct involvement in data theft, but does reveal they failed to report the analyst's stated intentions. A conduct failing rather than criminal activity.
partial.modifier: Director conduct issue identified. Regulatory notification may be required.

success.title: Clear Picture
success.description: The investigation clearly demonstrates the analyst acted alone and misrepresented the text messages (which were about the director's own career move). The analyst is referred to law enforcement.
success.modifier: Analyst's deception exposed. Clear basis for criminal referral.

critical_success.title: Critical Success — Justice Served
critical_success.description: The investigation not only clears the director but reveals the analyst attempted similar data theft at their previous employer. Law enforcement coordination leads to immediate arrest. The competing organisation launches its own internal investigation and terminates the headhunter.
critical_success.modifier: Criminal arrested. Pattern of behaviour established. Industry-wide alert issued.

random_complications: The analyst threatens to go to the press with allegations of a toxic work culture and whistleblower retaliation.
The director's executive assistant reveals they overheard conversations that partially corroborate the analyst's claims.
The organisation's internal investigation team reports to the director being investigated, creating an independence issue.
The sector regulator contacts the organisation about an unrelated matter, but the timing creates internal panic.

debrief.title: Post-Battle Assessment
questions: Were DLP controls effective in detecting the data exfiltration?
Was the investigation handled with appropriate legal and HR coordination?
How should privileged access controls be improved?
What training or awareness measures should be implemented?
